content: Understanding the NonNull pointer wrapper requires you to first grasp that Rust's memory model is built on a fundamental tension between safety and performance, where raw pointers represent the absolute truth of address space while higher-level abstractions attempt to shield programmers from errors. The NonNull<T> type acts as a critical bridge in this ecosystem by guaranteeing that the underlying pointer will never be null, effectively creating a semantic contract for functions that require a valid memory location but cannot afford the runtime overhead or semantic complexity of wrapping raw pointers in Option<T>. This guarantee is not merely documentation; it is enforced at compile time through the borrow checker's strict rules regarding nullability, ensuring that any function accepting a NonNull<T> parameter implicitly promises to never dereference a null address. The covariant nature of NonNull<T> means that if you have a reference to NonNull<i32>, you can safely treat it as a reference to NonNull<u64>, allowing for flexible memory management strategies where different integer types might share the same underlying storage allocation, which is particularly valuable when designing custom allocators or implementing unsafe collections like doubly linked lists where node pointers must always remain valid. The most subtle yet powerful feature of this construct emerges in the handling of zero-sized types, where calling the dangling() method on a NonNull<ZST> returns true without triggering undefined behavior; this optimization allows developers to distinguish between a legitimately empty collection and one that genuinely holds data by inspecting whether the pointer points to null rather than relying on expensive heap checks. In practice, you will frequently encounter the pattern of wrapping a raw pointer in NonNull<T> when implementing unsafe traits like Drop or when building custom allocators where memory must be reclaimed immediately after use without the possibility of accidental reuse due to dangling references. The danger lies in assuming that once a pointer is wrapped in NonNull<T>, it remains safe indefinitely; if you ever mutate the data behind the pointer and then attempt to dereference it again, you risk reintroducing nullability violations unless your logic strictly preserves the invariant that the memory location itself never becomes invalid or reallocated. When designing systems with complex ownership rules, remember that NonNull<T> does not provide the same level of safety as Box<T>; while Box<T> automatically manages lifetime and deallocation through drop semantics, NonNull<T> merely ensures the pointer exists without managing its lifecycle, meaning manual memory management errors remain your responsibility if you choose to use it in conjunction with custom allocators. The correct mental model is that NonNull<T> converts a potentially unsafe raw pointer into a safe abstraction only by contractually forbidding null checks, shifting the burden of safety from runtime validation to compile-time logic and developer discipline.

The most pervasive trap for anyone trying to understand Rusts memory safety is believing that a raw pointer or a smart pointer can ever truly be null in the way you might think of it in C. Beginners often write code like this: let ptr = SomeNonNull::new(alloc); if ptr.is_none() { /* do something */ }; and they get confused because they expect a non-null guarantee to somehow allow them to check for null without dropping the safety invariant. The fundamental misunderstanding is that NonNull<T> does not wrap an Option<T>; it wraps a raw pointer, but with an internal invariant that the pointer must never point to null (0). When you allocate memory using custom allocators or unsafe collections like your own Vec implementation, you are responsible for ensuring the underlying ptr is never null. If you try to create a NonNull<T> from a null pointer, the compiler will refuse to compile it because doing so would violate the safety contract of the type itself. This is why you see error E0492 about attempting to convert a nullable value into a non-nullable one; the language enforces this invariant at the type level so that you cannot accidentally introduce dangling pointers or use invalid memory addresses in your unsafe code.

The real power of NonNull<T> comes from its covariance, which is often overlooked but critical when dealing with generic collections. If you have a Vec<NonNull<i32>> and another Vec<NonNull<i64>>, Rust allows you to safely treat the latter as containing pointers similar to the former because NonNull<i64> can be used where NonNull<i32> is expected in many contexts due to how variance works with raw pointer representations. This matters immensely when designing custom allocators where you might want to store metadata alongside your data; by using a Vec<NonNull<T>> instead of a Vec<Option<RawPtr>>, you avoid the overhead of Option wrappers and ensure that every slot in your allocation is guaranteed to hold valid memory. You can also use the dangling method on NonNull<T> when working with zero-sized types or specific optimizations where you need to check if the pointer was ever actually allocated without violating the type system; however, remember that calling dangling() on a NonNull<T> that has been dropped will panic unless you have carefully managed your drop logic in unsafe code. The key insight is that NonNull<T> shifts the burden of null-checking onto the developer who creates it, forcing you to think about memory lifetimes and allocation boundaries immediately rather than deferring those checks to runtime conditions later. When implementing a custom allocator or building an unsafe collection like a Ring Buffer, you must ensure that every time you call new() on NonNull<T>, you are passing in a pointer that was successfully allocated from your pool; if you ever pass null, the program will never compile and you are saved from subtle runtime bugs where uninitialized memory causes crashes. This strictness is exactly why Rust designers chose this approach: it eliminates an entire class of undefined behavior related to dereferencing invalid pointers before you even write the first line of unsafe code.
